statement.markdown: Given an array of integers arr of size N, sort the array in ascending order using the Bubble Sort algorithm.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer N, representing the size of the array.
The second line contains N space-separated integers representing the array elements.
statement.outputFormat: A single line containing N space-separated integers representing the sorted array.

hints.text: The Bubble Sort algorithm involves comparing adjacent elements in the array and swapping them if they are in the wrong order. Start by thinking about how to iterate through the array to make these comparisons.

The algorithm needs to repeatedly go through the array, making swaps as necessary, until no more swaps are needed. This implies a loop that continues until a pass through the array results in no swaps being made.

A key insight into Bubble Sort is that with each pass through the array, the largest (or smallest, depending on sorting order) element 'bubbles' to the end (or beginning) of the array. This means that with each pass, the portion of the array that needs to be checked can be reduced.

editorial.title: Bubble Sort Analysis
editorial.intuition: The brute force approach to sorting an array involves comparing each pair of adjacent elements and swapping them if they are in the wrong order, which is the core idea behind bubble sort. This process is repeated until no more swaps are needed, indicating that the array is sorted.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force solution is correct but slow because it has a time complexity of O(n^2), where n is the number of elements in the array. This is due to the nested loops that compare and potentially swap each pair of elements. While this approach guarantees a sorted array, its inefficiency makes it impractical for large datasets.
editorial.betterSolution: An optimization to the basic bubble sort algorithm is to stop the algorithm if inner loop didn’t cause any swap, which means the list is already sorted.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution for bubble sort involves adding a flag that tracks whether any swaps were made during a pass through the array. If no swaps were made, the array is already sorted, and the algorithm can terminate early. This optimization reduces the number of unnecessary iterations, especially for nearly sorted or already sorted arrays.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of bubble sort can be proven by induction. After the first pass, the largest element is bubbled to the end of the array. After the second pass, the second-largest element is in its correct position, and so on. This process continues until the entire array is sorted.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the bubble sort algorithm on a small, unsorted array, such as [5, 2, 8, 3, 1], demonstrates how the algorithm repeatedly compares and swaps adjacent elements until the array is sorted, resulting in [1, 2, 3, 5, 8].
commonMistakes: Not checking for the case where the input array is already sorted
Not optimizing the algorithm to stop early when no swaps are made
Incorrectly implementing the swap operation between elements
Not handling edge cases such as an empty or single-element array
Using an incorrect loop condition that leads to out-of-bounds access
